{"code": "import json
import re

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print(f"Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}")

# Extract publication years from book details
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    
    text = str(details)
    # Look for month day, year format
    match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Look for any 4-digit year
    match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details'))
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(books_with_years)}")
print("Sample:", books_with_years[:5])

result = {
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'books_with_years': books_with_years[:10],
    'total_reviews': len(reviews)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load all books
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path) as f:
    books_json = f.read()
    books = json.loads(books_json)

# Load all reviews  
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews_json = f.read()
    reviews = json.loads(reviews_json)

print(f"Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}")

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = book.get('details', '') or ''
    
    year = None
    # Look for month day, year format
    match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', details, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
    else:
        # Look for any 4-digit year
        match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
    
    if year and 1900 <= year <= 2023:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(book_to_decade)}")
print("Sample mapping:", list(book_to_decade.items())[:5])

# Track ratings by decade
decade_books = {}  # Books per decade
decade_ratings = {}  # All ratings per decade

for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Convert purchase_id to book_id
    book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_')
    
    if book_id in book_to_decade:
        decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
        
        # Track distinct books
        if decade not in decade_books:
            decade_books[decade] = set()
        decade_books[decade].add(book_id)
        
        # Track ratings
        if decade not in decade_ratings:
            decade_ratings[decade] = []
        decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)

# Calculate averages for decades with at least 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade in decade_ratings:
    distinct_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(decade_ratings[decade]) / len(decade_ratings[decade])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'total_reviews': len(decade_ratings[decade])
        })

# Sort by average rating
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"\nDecades with >= 10 distinct books: {len(results)}")
for r in results:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}: avg={r['avg_rating']}, books={r['distinct_books']}, reviews={r['total_reviews']}")

if results:
    best_decade = results[0]['decade']
    print(f"\nBest decade: {best_decade}")
else:
    best_decade = "No qualifying decade"

result = {
    'best_decade': best_decade,
    'all_results': results
}
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

        year = None
        # Look for "Month 1, 2020" format
        match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', str(details))
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
        else:
            # Look for any year 1900-2023
            match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', str(details))
            if match:
                year = int(match.group(1))
        
        if year and 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            book_id_to_decade[book_id] = decade

# Aggregate ratings by decade
decade_ratings = {}  # decade -> list of ratings
decade_books = {}    # decade -> set of book_ids

for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Convert purchase_id to book_id
    if purchase_id.startswith('purchaseid_'):
        book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_', 1)
        
        if book_id in book_id_to_decade:
            decade = book_id_to_decade[book_id]
            
            if decade not in decade_ratings:
                decade_ratings[decade] = []
                decade_books[decade] = set()
            
            decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)
            decade_books[decade].add(book_id)

# Find best decade with at least 10 distinct books
best_decade = None
best_avg_rating = 0
all_results = []

for decade in decade_ratings:
    distinct_books_count = len(decade_books[decade])
    if distinct_books_count >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(decade_ratings[decade]) / len(decade_ratings[decade])
        all_results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'distinct_books': distinct_books_count
        })
        
        if avg_rating > best_avg_rating:
            best_decade = decade
            best_avg_rating = avg_rating

# Sort results
all_results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"Found {len(all_results)} decades with >= 10 distinct books")
for r in all_results:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}: Avg={r['avg_rating']}, Books={r['distinct_books']}")

result = {
    'best_decade': best_decade if best_decade else 'No qualifying decade found',
    'rankings': all_results
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json

# Load the book details data
with open('var_functions.query_db:8') as f:
    book_details = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrame
df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_details)

# Extract year from details using regex
import re

def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Look for 4-digit years (1900-2023)
    patterns = [
        r'published[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'released[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'January[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'February[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'March[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'April[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'May[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'June[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'July[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'August[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'September[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'October[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'November[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'December[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'  # Any 4-digit year from 1900-2029
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Calculate decade
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(lambda x: f"{x // 10 * 10}s" if pd.notna(x) else None)

# Show summary
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'books_with_year': df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum(),
    'sample_books': df_books[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records')
}))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Get the book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Create a more robust year extraction function
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    # Try multiple patterns in order of likelihood
    patterns = [
        # Pattern for dates like "May 20, 1996" or "January 1, 2004"
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "published on 2012"
        r'published[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "released on 2012"
        r'released[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "edition on 2012"
        r'edition[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Any 4-digit year from 1900-2023
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if matches:
            # For the first pattern, we get month and year, so take the year (second group)
            if 'month' in pattern:
                for match in matches:
                    if isinstance(match, tuple) and len(match) == 2:
                        year = int(match[1])
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
            else:
                # For other patterns, we get just the year
                for match in matches:
                    if isinstance(match, str):
                        year = int(match)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
                    elif isinstance(match, tuple):
                        year_str = match[0] if match[0] else match[1] if len(match) > 1 else match[0]
                        year = int(year_str)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Look at some examples that failed before
failed_before = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].isna()].head(10)
print("Examples of books without extracted year:")
for idx, row in failed_before.head(5).iterrows():
    print(f"\nBook ID: {row['book_id']}")
    print(f"Details: {row['details'][:200]}...")

# Also check successful ones
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"\n\nSuccessfully extracted years for {len(successful)} books:")
for idx, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"Book ID: {row['book_id']}, Year: {row['publication_year']}")"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Get the book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# More robust year extraction
def extract_year_new(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    # Direct year patterns - most common
    patterns = [
        # Month day, year format
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(?:\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Words followed by year
        r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,30}(\d{4})',
        # Just a year
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, str(details_text), re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year_str = match.group(1)
            try:
                year = int(year_str)
                if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                    return year
            except:
                continue
    
    return None

# Apply the extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_new)

# Show results
books_with_year = df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()
print(f"Total books: {len(df_books)}")
print(f"Books with extracted year: {books_with_year}")

# Show successful examples
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
for idx, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"Book {row['book_id']}: Year {row['publication_year']}")

# Show failed examples
failed = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].isna()].head(3)
print("\nFailed examples:")
for idx, row in failed.iterrows():
    print(f"Book {row['book_id']}: {row['details'][:150]}...")"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load the book details data (file path from previous query)
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Robust year extraction function
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    details_str = str(details_text)
    
    # Pattern 1: Month day, year (e.g., "January 1, 2004")
    pattern1 = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match1 = re.search(pattern1, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        year = int(match1.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 2: Words followed by year (e.g., "published on May 18, 2014")
    pattern2 = r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,50}(\d{4})'
    match2 = re.search(pattern2, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        year = int(match2.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 3: Any year from 1900-2023
    pattern3 = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match3 = re.search(pattern3, details_str)
    if match3:
        year = int(match3.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract publication years
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Calculate decade
def get_decade(year):
    if pd.isna(year):
        return None
    return f"{int(year) // 10 * 10}s"

df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(get_decade)

# Verify extraction with sample results
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"Successfully extracted years for {len(successful)} out of {len(df_books)} books")
print("\nSample extractions:")
for idx, row in successful.head(15).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

# Check if we need to get more books or if this is sufficient
if len(successful) < 50:
    print("\nWarning: Less than 50 books with extracted years. Getting all books...")
    need_more = True
else:
    need_more = False

result_data = {
    'books_with_year': len(successful),
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'sample_books': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records'),
    'need_more_books': need_more
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result_data, default=str))"}

{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

books_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Extract publication year from details
def extract_year(details_str):
    if not details_str:
        return None
    
    # Try to extract 4-digit year patterns
    patterns = [
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(?:\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, str(details_str), re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(lambda x: f"{x//10*10}s" if x else None)

# Show results
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"Books with year: {len(successful)}")
print("Example extractions:")
for _, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

result = {
    'count': len(successful),
    'books': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].to_dict('records')
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load the complete book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

print(f"Total books in dataset: {len(df_books)}")

# Robust year extraction
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    details_str = str(details_text)
    
    # Pattern 1: Month day, year format (e.g., "January 1, 2004")
    pattern1 = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match1 = re.search(pattern1, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        year = int(match1.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 2: Published/released/edition with year nearby
    pattern2 = r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,80}(\d{4})'
    match2 = re.search(pattern2, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        year = int(match2.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 3: Any 4-digit year from 1900-2023 as fallback
    pattern3 = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match3 = re.search(pattern3, details_str)
    if match3:
        year = int(match3.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Calculate decade
def get_decade(year):
    if pd.isna(year) or year is None:
        return None
    return f"{int(year) // 10 * 10}s"

df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(get_decade)

# Summary of year extraction
books_with_year = df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()
print(f"Books with extracted publication year: {books_with_year}")

# Show some examples
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"\nExample extractions:")
for idx, row in successful.head(20).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

# Save for next steps
result_info = {
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'books_with_year': int(books_with_year),
    'decade_sample': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records')
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result_info))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path) as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Extract years from book details
def extract_year_from_details(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    text = str(details_text)
    
    # Look for month day, year format first
    month_pattern = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match = re.search(month_pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Look for any year 1900-2023 as fallback
    year_pattern = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match = re.search(year_pattern, text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Process all books
books_with_years = []
total_books = len(books_data)

for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year_from_details(book.get('details'))
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Processed {total_books} books")
print(f"Found publication years for {len(books_with_years)} books")

# Show some examples
print("\nSample books with extracted years:")
for i, book in enumerate(books_with_years[:10]):
    print(f"  {book['book_id']}: {book['year']} ({book['decade']})")

result = {
    'total_books_processed': total_books,
    'books_with_years': books_with_years,
    'count': len(books_with_years)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Although we've been here for 20 years, I would say that it's only been within the past five years that this former city girl has embraced the country lifestyle. That lifestyle includes hunting, something that I've almost lost some city friends over.

My feelings about hunting are as follows...although I myself eat very little meat, my dogs are my family, and what can I say, it's a family of carnivores! In addition, when you are aware of the dark side of those pretty plastic wrapped packages of meat in the supermarket, hunting seems pretty darn humane, and a reasonable way to procure meat for your family. I think of what the cows and chickens made into dog kibble went through, and what diseases they might have had before being in a can of cat food.

That being said, I'm always interested in different perspectives about hunting, lifestyles, and philosophies.

I enjoyed this book quite a bit because it contains all of the above. There seem to be so many books about hunting, magazines about hunting. Books on bow hunting, and dialogue about hunting a mule deer as opposed to a white tailed deer. Because I am passionate about knowing where my food comes from, and doing even a small part to make choices that are responsible, it was refreshing and affirming to read the author's perspective. When we grab a fast food hamburger, we don't think about the suffering animal that probably led an unnatural life, and we certainly don't think of the workers in the processing plants that do a job so brutal and often dangerous. {"code": "import json
import re

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print(f"Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}")

# Extract publication years from book details
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    
    text = str(details)
    # Look for month day, year format
    match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Look for any 4-digit year
    match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details'))
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(books_with_years)}")
print("Sample:", books_with_years[:5])

result = {
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'books_with_years': books_with_years[:10],
    'total_reviews': len(reviews)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load all books
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path) as f:
    books_json = f.read()
    books = json.loads(books_json)

# Load all reviews  
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews_json = f.read()
    reviews = json.loads(reviews_json)

print(f"Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}")

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = book.get('details', '') or ''
    
    year = None
    # Look for month day, year format
    match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', details, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
    else:
        # Look for any 4-digit year
        match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
    
    if year and 1900 <= year <= 2023:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(book_to_decade)}")
print("Sample mapping:", list(book_to_decade.items())[:5])

# Track ratings by decade
decade_books = {}  # Books per decade
decade_ratings = {}  # All ratings per decade

for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Convert purchase_id to book_id
    book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_')
    
    if book_id in book_to_decade:
        decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
        
        # Track distinct books
        if decade not in decade_books:
            decade_books[decade] = set()
        decade_books[decade].add(book_id)
        
        # Track ratings
        if decade not in decade_ratings:
            decade_ratings[decade] = []
        decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)

# Calculate averages for decades with at least 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade in decade_ratings:
    distinct_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(decade_ratings[decade]) / len(decade_ratings[decade])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'total_reviews': len(decade_ratings[decade])
        })

# Sort by average rating
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Load books data
books_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']
with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(books)} books and {len(reviews)} reviews")

# Extract good publication years
book_to_decade = {}
good_years = 0

for book in books:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = book.get('details')
    
    if details:  # Check if details exists and is not null
        year = None
        # First try: month day, year format
        match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', str(details))
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
        else:
            # Second try: any 4-digit year
            match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', str(details))
            if match:
                year = int(match.group(1))
        
        if year and 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
            book_to_decade[book_id] = decade
            good_years += 1

print(f"Books with valid publication years: {good_years}")

# Group reviews by decade
decade_data = {}

for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Convert purchase_id to book_id
    book_id = 'bookid_' + purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', '', 1)
    
    if book_id in book_to_decade:
        decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
        
        if decade not in decade_data:
            decade_data[decade] = {'books': set(), 'ratings': []}
        
        decade_data[decade]['books'].add(book_id)
        decade_data[decade]['ratings'].append(rating)

# Calculate averages for decades with >= 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade, data in decade_data.items():
    distinct_books = len(data['books'])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg = sum(data['ratings']) / len(data['ratings'])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg, 3),
            'distinct_books': distinct_books
        })

# Sort by rating
top_decades = sorted(results, key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

best = top_decades[0] if top_decades else None

print("Decades with >= 10 books:")
for d in top_decades:
    print(f"  {d['decade']}: {d['avg_rating']} (books: {d['distinct_books']})")

output = {
    'best_decade': best['decade'] if best else 'None found',
    'rankings': top_decades
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(output))"}

This would avoid the Python processing issues entirely.

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Review Title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_info table in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_info.

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json

# Load the book details data
with open('var_functions.query_db:8') as f:
    book_details = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrame
df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_details)

# Extract year from details using regex
import re

def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Look for 4-digit years (1900-2023)
    patterns = [
        r'published[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'released[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'January[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'February[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'March[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'April[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'May[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'June[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'July[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'August[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'September[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'October[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'November[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'December[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'  # Any 4-digit year from 1900-2029
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Calculate decade
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(lambda x: f"{x // 10 * 10}s" if pd.notna(x) else None)

# Show summary
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'books_with_year': df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum(),
    'sample_books': df_books[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records')
}))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Get the book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Create a more robust year extraction function
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    # Try multiple patterns in order of likelihood
    patterns = [
        # Pattern for dates like "May 20, 1996" or "January 1, 2004"
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "published on 2012"
        r'published[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "released on 2012"
        r'released[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "edition on 2012"
        r'edition[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Any 4-digit year from 1900-2023
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if matches:
            # For the first pattern, we get month and year, so take the year (second group)
            if 'month' in pattern:
                for match in matches:
                    if isinstance(match, tuple) and len(match) == 2:
                        year = int(match[1])
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
            else:
                # For other patterns, we get just the year
                for match in matches:
                    if isinstance(match, str):
                        year = int(match)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
                    elif isinstance(match, tuple):
                        year_str = match[0] if match[0] else match[1] if len(match) > 1 else match[0]
                        year = int(year_str)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Look at some examples that failed before
failed_before = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].isna()].head(10)
print("Examples of books without extracted year:")
for idx, row in failed_before.head(5).iterrows():
    print(f"\nBook ID: {row['book_id']}")
    print(f"Details: {row['details'][:200]}...")

# Also check successful ones
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"\n\nSuccessfully extracted years for {len(successful)} books:")
for idx, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"Book ID: {row['book_id']}, Year: {row['publication_year']}")"}

{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Get the book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:8']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# More robust year extraction
def extract_year_new(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    # Direct year patterns - most common
    patterns = [
        # Month day, year format
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(?:\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Words followed by year
        r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,30}(\d{4})',
        # Just a year
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, str(details_text), re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year_str = match.group(1)
            try:
                year = int(year_str)
                if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                    return year
            except:
                continue
    
    return None

# Apply the extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_new)

# Show results
books_with_year = df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()
print(f"Total books: {len(df_books)}")
print(f"Books with extracted year: {books_with_year}")

# Show successful examples
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
for idx, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"Book {row['book_id']}: Year {row['publication_year']}")

# Show failed examples
failed = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].isna()].head(3)
print("\nFailed examples:")
for idx, row in failed.iterrows():
    print(f"Book {row['book_id']}: {row['details'][:150]}...")"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load the book details data (file path from previous query)
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Robust year extraction function
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    details_str = str(details_text)
    
    # Pattern 1: Month day, year (e.g., "January 1, 2004")
    pattern1 = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match1 = re.search(pattern1, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        year = int(match1.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 2: Words followed by year (e.g., "published on May 18, 2014")
    pattern2 = r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,50}(\d{4})'
    match2 = re.search(pattern2, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        year = int(match2.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 3: Any year from 1900-2023
    pattern3 = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match3 = re.search(pattern3, details_str)
    if match3:
        year = int(match3.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract publication years
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Calculate decade
def get_decade(year):
    if pd.isna(year):
        return None
    return f"{int(year) // 10 * 10}s"

df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(get_decade)

# Verify extraction with sample results
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"Successfully extracted years for {len(successful)} out of {len(df_books)} books")
print("\nSample extractions:")
for idx, row in successful.head(15).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

# Check if we need to get more books or if this is sufficient
if len(successful) < 50:
    print("\nWarning: Less than 50 books with extracted years. Getting all books...")
    need_more = True
else:
    need_more = False

result_data = {
    'books_with_year': len(successful),
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'sample_books': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records'),
    'need_more_books': need_more
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result_data, default=str))"}

import re

books_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Extract publication year from details
def extract_year(details_str):
    if not details_str:
        return None
    
    # Try to extract 4-digit year patterns
    patterns = [
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(?:\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, str(details_str), re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(lambda x: f"{x//10*10}s" if x else None)

# Show results
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"Books with year: {len(successful)}")
print("Example extractions:")
for _, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

result = {
    'count': len(successful),
    'books': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].to_dict('records')
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load the complete book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

print(f"Total books in dataset: {len(df_books)}")

# Robust year extraction
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    details_str = str(details_text)
    
    # Pattern 1: Month day, year format (e.g., "January 1, 2004")
    pattern1 = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match1 = re.search(pattern1, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        year = int(match1.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 2: Published/released/edition with year nearby
    pattern2 = r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,80}(\d{4})'
    match2 = re.search(pattern2, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        year = int(match2.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 3: Any 4-digit year from 1900-2023 as fallback
    pattern3 = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match3 = re.search(pattern3, details_str)
    if match3:
        year = int(match3.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Calculate decade
def get_decade(year):
    if pd.isna(year) or year is None:
        return None
    return f"{int(year) // 10 * 10}s"

df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(get_decade)

# Summary of year extraction
books_with_year = df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()
print(f"Books with extracted publication year: {books_with_year}")

# Show some examples
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"\nExample extractions:")
for idx, row in successful.head(20).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

# Save for next steps
result_info = {
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'books_with_year': int(books_with_year),
    'decade_sample': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records')
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result_info))"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path) as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Extract years from book details
def extract_year_from_details(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    text = str(details_text)
    
    # Look for month day, year format first
    month_pattern = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match = re.search(month_pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Look for any year 1900-2023 as fallback
    year_pattern = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match = re.search(year_pattern, text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Process all books
books_with_years = []
total_books = len(books_data)

for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year_from_details(book.get('details'))
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Processed {total_books} books")
print(f"Found publication years for {len(books_with_years)} books")

# Show some examples
print("\nSample books with extracted years:")
for i, book in enumerate(books_with_years[:10]):
    print(f"  {book['book_id']}: {book['year']} ({book['decade']})")

result = {
    'total_books_processed': total_books,
    'books_with_years': books_with_years,
    'count': len(books_with_years)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Although we've been here for 20 years, I would say that it's only been within the past five years that this former city girl has embraced the country lifestyle. That lifestyle includes hunting, something that I've almost lost some city friends over.

My feelings about hunting are as follows...although I myself eat very little meat, my dogs are my family, and what can I say, it's a family of carnivores! In addition, when you are aware of the dark side of those pretty plastic wrapped packages of meat in the supermarket, hunting seems pretty darn humane, and a reasonable way to procure meat for your family. I think of what the cows and chickens made into dog kibble went through, and what diseases they might have had before being in a can of cat food.

That being said, I'm always interested in different perspectives about hunting, lifestyles, and philosophies.

I enjoyed this book quite a bit because it contains all of the above. There seem to be so many books about hunting, magazines about hunting. Books on bow hunting, and dialogue about hunting a mule deer as opposed to a white tailed deer. I felt like this book fills a gap that had not been covered in any of the articles or books I had previously read.

Because I am passionate about knowing where my food comes from, and doing even a small part to make choices that are responsible, it was refreshing and affirming to read the author's perspective. When we grab a fast food hamburger, we don't think about the suffering animal that probably led an unnatural life, and we certainly don't think of the workers in the processing plants that do a job so brutal and often dangerous. This book reminds us to respect the food we eat, something that comes more naturally when you are closer to your food, closer to the animal that gives its life.", "review_time": "2013-02-20 16:09:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Greet book", "text": "Lots of great information. But now this guy is living in NYC, so I can imagine how he's now surrounded by people so far removed from his reality that they think he's a barbarian (similar to my Jersey-raised brother-in-law who once said to me in surprise, \"they let you have guns!?!?!\", as if he grew up under some communist regime rather than in the Land of the Free).

And I want to be clear that he is a very talented writer and it was very easy to enjoy this book, even not being an active hunter myself. He reminds me a little bit of myself in that he can write and explain his feelings eloquently, and yet if you don't like him without justification, he'll become self righteous quickly and give you more reasons to dislike him. You'll see his writing have spots of crudeness at times, which allows you to see where he grew up.

None of these are meant to be negative. I'm just trying - perhaps imperfectly - to point out why this is a strange book. function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re

# Load books data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
with open(reviews_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print(f"Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}")

# Extract publication years from book details
def extract_year(details):
    if not details:
        return None
    
    text = str(details)
    # Look for month day, year format
    match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Look for any 4-digit year
    match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years for books
books_with_years = []
for book in books:
    year = extract_year(book.get('details'))
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(books_with_years)}")
print("Sample:", books_with_years[:5])

result = {
    'books_with_years_count': len(books_with_years),
    'books_with_years': books_with_years[:10],
    'total_reviews': len(reviews)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

    reviews_json = f.read()
    reviews = json.loads(reviews_json)

print(f"Books: {len(books)}, Reviews: {len(reviews)}")

# Create mapping from book_id to decade
book_to_decade = {}
for book in books:
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = book.get('details', '') or ''
    
    year = None
    # Look for month day, year format
    match = re.search(r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})', details, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
    else:
        # Look for any 4-digit year
        match = re.search(r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b', details)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
    
    if year and 1900 <= year <= 2023:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        book_to_decade[book_id] = decade

print(f"Books with publication years: {len(book_to_decade)}")
print("Sample mapping:", list(book_to_decade.items())[:5])

# Track ratings by decade
decade_books = {}  # Books per decade
decade_ratings = {}  # All ratings per decade

for review in reviews:
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    
    # Convert purchase_id to book_id
    book_id = purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', 'bookid_')
    
    if book_id in book_to_decade:
        decade = book_to_decade[book_id]
        
        # Track distinct books
        if decade not in decade_books:
            decade_books[decade] = set()
        decade_books[decade].add(book_id)
        
        # Track ratings
        if decade not in decade_ratings:
            decade_ratings[decade] = []
        decade_ratings[decade].append(rating)

# Calculate averages for decades with at least 10 distinct books
results = []
for decade in decade_ratings:
    distinct_books = len(decade_books[decade])
    if distinct_books >= 10:
        avg_rating = sum(decade_ratings[decade]) / len(decade_ratings[decade])
        results.append({
            'decade': decade,
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 3),
            'distinct_books': distinct_books,
            'total_reviews': len(decade_ratings[decade])
        })

# Sort by average rating
results.sort(key=lambda x: x['avg_rating'], reverse=True)

print(f"\nDecades with >= 10 distinct books: {len(results)}")
for r in results:
    print(f"  {r['decade']}: avg={r['avg_rating']}, books={r['distinct_books']}, reviews={r['total_reviews']}")

if results:
    best_decade = results[0]['decade']
    print(f"\nBest decade: {best_decade}")
else:
    best_decade = "No qualifying decade"

result = {
    'best_decade': best_decade,
    'all_results': results
}
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

QUERY:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

2. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:

        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)

        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json

# Load the book details data
with open('var_functions.query_db:8') as f:
    book_details = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrame
df_books = pd.DataFrame(book_details)

# Extract year from details using regex
import re

def extract_year(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    # Look for 4-digit years (1900-2023)
    patterns = [
        r'published[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'released[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'January[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'February[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'March[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'April[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'May[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'June[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'July[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'August[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'September[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'October[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'November[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'December[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'  # Any 4-digit year from 1900-2029
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Calculate decade
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(lambda x: f"{x // 10 * 10}s" if pd.notna(x) else None)

# Show summary
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'books_with_year': df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum(),
    'sample_books': df_books[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records')
}))"}

with open(file_path, 'r') as f:

# Create a more robust year extraction function
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    # Try multiple patterns in order of likelihood
    patterns = [
        # Pattern for dates like "May 20, 1996" or "January 1, 2004"
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "published on 2012"
        r'published[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "released on 2012"
        r'released[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Pattern for "edition on 2012"
        r'edition[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        # Any 4-digit year from 1900-2023
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        matches = re.findall(pattern, details_text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if matches:
            # For the first pattern, we get month and year, so take the year (second group)
            if 'month' in pattern:
                for match in matches:

                        year = int(match[1])
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:

            else:
                # For other patterns, we get just the year
                for match in matches:
                    if isinstance(match, str):
                        year = int(match)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
                    elif isinstance(match, tuple):
                        year_str = match[0] if match[0] else match[1] if len(match) > 1 else match[0]
                        year = int(year_str)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction

function.arguments:
{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load the book details data (file path from previous query)
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:22']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Robust year extraction function
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    details_str = str(details_text)
    
    # Pattern 1: Month day, year (e.g., "January 1, 2004")
    pattern1 = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match1 = re.search(pattern1, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        year = int(match1.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 2: Words followed by year (e.g., "published on May 18, 2014")
    pattern2 = r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,50}(\d{4})'
    match2 = re.search(pattern2, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        year = int(match2.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 3: Any year from 1900-2023
    pattern3 = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match3 = re.search(pattern3, details_str)
    if match3:
        year = int(match3.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract publication years
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Calculate decade
def get_decade(year):
    if pd.isna(year):
        return None
    return f"{int(year) // 10 * 10}s"

df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(get_decade)

# Verify extraction with sample results
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"Successfully extracted years for {len(successful)} out of {len(df_books)} books")
print("\nSample extractions:")
for idx, row in successful.head(15).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

# Check if we need to get more books or if this is sufficient
if len(successful) < 50:
    print("\nWarning: Less than 50 books with extracted years. Getting all books...")
    need_more = True
else:
    need_more = False

result_data = {
    'books_with_year': len(successful),
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'sample_books': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records'),
    'need_more_books': need_more
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result_data, default=str))"}

import re

# Extract publication year from details

    if not details_str:
        return None
    
    # Try to extract 4-digit year patterns
    patterns = [
        r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*(?:\d{1,2})[^\d]*(\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, str(details_str), re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            year = int(match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                return year
    
    return None

# Apply extraction
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(lambda x: f"{x//10*10}s" if x else None)

# Show results
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"Books with year: {len(successful)}")
print("Example extractions:")
for _, row in successful.head(10).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

result = {
    'count': len(successful),
    'books': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].to_dict('records')
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

{"code": "import pandas as pd
import json
import re

# Load the complete book data
file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

print(f"Total books in dataset: {len(df_books)}")

# Robust year extraction
def extract_year_robust(details_text):
    if not details_text or pd.isna(details_text):
        return None
    
    details_str = str(details_text)
    
    # Pattern 1: Month day, year format (e.g., "January 1, 2004")
    pattern1 = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match1 = re.search(pattern1, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        year = int(match1.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 2: Published/released/edition with year nearby
    pattern2 = r'(?:published|released|edition)[\s\S]{0,80}(\d{4})'
    match2 = re.search(pattern2, details_str, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        year = int(match2.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Pattern 3: Any 4-digit year from 1900-2023 as fallback
    pattern3 = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match3 = re.search(pattern3, details_str)
    if match3:
        year = int(match3.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Extract years
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['details'].apply(extract_year_robust)

# Calculate decade
def get_decade(year):
    if pd.isna(year) or year is None:
        return None
    return f"{int(year) // 10 * 10}s"

df_books['decade'] = df_books['publication_year'].apply(get_decade)

# Summary of year extraction
books_with_year = df_books['publication_year'].notna().sum()
print(f"Books with extracted publication year: {books_with_year}")

# Show some examples
successful = df_books[df_books['publication_year'].notna()]
print(f"\nExample extractions:")
for idx, row in successful.head(20).iterrows():
    print(f"  {row['book_id']}: {row['publication_year']} ({row['decade']})")

# Save for next steps
result_info = {
    'total_books': len(df_books),
    'books_with_year': int(books_with_year),
    'decade_sample': successful[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade']].head(10).to_dict('records')
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result_info))"}

{"code": "import json
import re

# Load the data
books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:28']
with open(books_path) as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Extract years from book details
def extract_year_from_details(details_text):
    if not details_text:
        return None
    
    text = str(details_text)
    
    # Look for month day, year format first
    month_pattern = r'(?:January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)[^\d]*\d{1,2}[^\d]*(\d{4})'
    match = re.search(month_pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    # Look for any year 1900-2023 as fallback
    year_pattern = r'\b(19\d{2}|20[0-2]\d)\b'
    match = re.search(year_pattern, text)
    if match:
        year = int(match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            return year
    
    return None

# Process all books
books_with_years = []
total_books = len(books_data)

for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year_from_details(book.get('details'))
    if year:
        decade = f"{year//10*10}s"
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'year': year,
            'decade': decade
        })

print(f"Processed {total_books} books")
print(f"Found publication years for {len(books_with_years)} books")

# Show some examples
print("\nSample books with extracted years:")
for i, book in enumerate(books_with_years[:10]):
    print(f"  {book['book_id']}: {book['year']} ({book['decade']})")

result = {
    'total_books_processed': total_books,
    'books_with_years': books_with_years,
    'count': len(books_with_years)
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))"}

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did.
